events.text: The patient is a 58-year-old female named Margaret Okonkwo. She is of West African descent, postmenopausal, gravida 3 para 3, non-smoker, with a family history significant for breast cancer in her mother (diagnosed at age 45) and ovarian cancer in a maternal aunt. She has a history of hypertension managed with lisinopril and a remote history of appendectomy.

At age 58 years, the patient had a diagnosis of FIGO Stage IIIC (AJCC 8th edition pT3c pN1 M0) HIGH-GRADE SEROUS CARCINOMA OF THE OVARY (ICD-O-3 site code C56.9, histology code 8461/3: Serous cystadenocarcinoma, NOS). Bilateral ovarian involvement with omental cake and diffuse peritoneal carcinomatosis including diaphragmatic implants. Ascites volume estimated at 3.2 liters on diagnostic paracentesis. CA-125 at diagnosis: 4,820 U/mL. HE4: 312 pmol/L. ROMA score: 94.3% (high risk). Confirmation: POSITIVE HISTOLOGY. Tumor grade: Grade 3 (high-grade). Tumor size: bilateral ovarian masses, right 8.4 cm, left 5.1 cm. ER/PR: ER positive (30%), PR negative. No lymphovascular invasion identified on biopsy specimen. Performance status: ECOG 1.

At age 58 years, the patient had a CT scan of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis with contrast (diagnostic staging), which showed cancer. Bilateral ovarian masses: right ovary 8.4 x 6.2 x 5.8 cm, left ovary 5.1 x 4.0 x 3.7 cm with internal septations and solid components. Large volume ascites. Diffuse peritoneal carcinomatosis with studding along the paracolic gutters, liver capsule (Glisson's capsule implants without parenchymal involvement), diaphragmatic surfaces bilaterally, and omentum (omental cake 9.2 x 4.8 cm). Pelvic sidewall adenopathy up to 1.6 cm. No pleural effusion. No hepatic parenchymal metastases. No pulmonary metastases. Impression: Findings highly suspicious for advanced ovarian carcinoma with peritoneal carcinomatosis; no supradiaphragmatic disease. Peritoneal Cancer Index (estimated): 18.

At age 58 years, the patient had a pathology result of type DIAGNOSTIC ANATOMIC PATHOLOGY (omental biopsy and peritoneal washing obtained by image-guided core needle biopsy). Omental core biopsy: High-grade serous carcinoma. Morphology: sheets and nests of highly atypical epithelial cells with marked nuclear pleomorphism, prominent nucleoli, abundant mitoses (>10/hpf), and psammoma bodies. Immunohistochemistry: PAX8 positive, WT-1 positive, p53 aberrant (overexpression pattern, diffuse strong positivity >90% of cells), CK7 positive, CK20 negative, ER positive (30%), PR negative, Her2 negative. Ki-67: 80%. Peritoneal washing: Positive for malignant cells consistent with high-grade serous carcinoma. Diagnosis: High-grade serous carcinoma of Mullerian origin, consistent with primary ovarian/peritoneal origin.

At age 58 years, the patient had next-generation sequencing performed for high-grade serous ovarian carcinoma based on a specimen obtained from an omental biopsy (peritoneal implant). Germline testing (blood): BRCA1 pathogenic variant c.5266dupC (p.Gln1756ProfsTer74), a frameshift mutation in exon 20 associated with hereditary breast and ovarian cancer syndrome. Somatic tumor sequencing: TP53 p.R175H missense mutation (VAF 68%), consistent with loss-of-function; BRCA1 somatic LOH present (second allele lost); RB1 splice site mutation c.1386+1G>A (VAF 22%); CCNE1 amplification (copy number 8.4, high-level amplification); MYC gain (copy number 3.8); NF1 frameshift p.Q1966fs (VAF 19%); RAD51C promoter hypermethylation absent; CDK12 wild-type; PTEN wild-type; PIK3CA wild-type; KRAS wild-type; NRAS wild-type; BRAF wild-type. HRD score: 48 (HRD positive, threshold >42). Genomic LOH: 18.2% (elevated). Tumor mutational burden (TMB): 4.2 mut/Mb (low). Microsatellite instability (MSI): MSS (microsatellite stable). Actionable alterations: BRCA1 pathogenic germline variant (PARP inhibitor sensitivity, Tier IA); HRD positive (PARP inhibitor benefit, Tier IB). Relevant co-mutations: TP53 (expected for HGSOC), CCNE1 amplification (may predict relative resistance to platinum; prognostic significance uncertain in BRCA1-mutated context).

At age 58 years, the patient had a gynecologic oncology consultation and pre-surgical assessment. Multidisciplinary tumor board reviewed case. Given CT imaging suggesting potentially resectable disease without bulky retroperitoneal nodes above the renal vessels and no pleural effusions, the patient was considered a candidate for primary debulking surgery (PDS) with goal of R0 resection. Diagnostic laparoscopy performed first to assess resectability: Fagotti score calculated at 6 (bilateral ovarian masses +2, omental cake +2, diaphragmatic involvement +2; no bowel mesentery involvement, no stomach involvement, no liver surface score). Fagotti score of 6 was considered borderline; multidisciplinary discussion concluded that PDS should be attempted by an experienced gynecologic oncologist given absence of unresectable upper abdominal disease. Patient consented to possible bowel resection. Anesthesia pre-assessment: ASA class II. Nutritional assessment: albumin 3.4 g/dL, pre-surgical optimization with nutritional support initiated.

At age 58 years, the patient underwent PRIMARY DEBULKING SURGERY (PDS) via midline laparotomy. Procedure: Total abdominal hysterectomy, bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy, omentectomy (infracolic and supracolic), bilateral pelvic and para-aortic lymph node dissection, peritoneal stripping of bilateral diaphragmatic surfaces, peritonectomy of right paracolic gutter, rectosigmoid resection with primary anastomosis (colorectal stapled anastomosis, no diverting ostomy), appendectomy, cholecystectomy (incidental gallstone noted), and splenectomy (splenic hilum implant). Estimated blood loss: 1,850 mL. Intraoperative transfusion: 2 units packed red blood cells. Operative time: 7 hours 42 minutes. Surgical findings: Bilateral ovarian masses as described, omental cake, diaphragmatic studding bilaterally (right > left), splenic hilum implant, 4 pelvic sidewall implants, 3 para-aortic implants, rectosigmoid serosal implant requiring bowel resection. Residual disease at end of surgery: R0 (no gross residual disease). Surgeon: Dr. Adebimpe Adeyemi, Gynecologic Oncology. Pathologic specimen sent for final assessment.

At age 58 years, the patient had a pathology result of type SURGICAL PATHOLOGY (primary debulking surgery specimen). Right ovary and fallopian tube: High-grade serous carcinoma, 8.4 cm, with extension through ovarian surface; fallopian tube mucosa involved (STIC - serous tubal intraepithelial carcinoma identified in right fallopian tube, consistent with primary tubal origin). Left ovary and fallopian tube: High-grade serous carcinoma, 5.1 cm, with extension through ovarian surface. Uterus: Serosal implants of high-grade serous carcinoma; endometrium and myometrium uninvolved. Omentum: Extensive involvement by high-grade serous carcinoma (omental cake). Diaphragm (right peritoneal strip): High-grade serous carcinoma, multifocal. Peritoneum (right paracolic): High-grade serous carcinoma, multifocal. Rectosigmoid: Serosal and subserosal involvement; mucosa uninvolved; margins negative. Spleen: Splenic hilar soft tissue with high-grade serous carcinoma; splenic parenchyma uninvolved. Pelvic lymph nodes: 4/18 positive for metastatic high-grade serous carcinoma (largest deposit 1.4 cm). Para-aortic lymph nodes: 2/14 positive. Appendix: No tumor. Final pathologic stage: pT3c pN1 M0, FIGO IIIC.

At age 58 years, the patient experienced a post-operative complication of ILEUS (CTCAE Grade 2) on post-operative day 4, requiring nasogastric tube placement and NPO status. Hospital stay extended by 4 days. Managed conservatively with bowel rest, IV fluids, ambulation. Resolution by post-operative day 8. No anastomotic leak identified on Gastrografin enema performed on POD 7. Additional post-operative complication: WOUND SEROMA (CTCAE Grade 1) at midline incision, managed with observation. Patient discharged on post-operative day 12 in stable condition.

At age 58 years (approximately 5 weeks post-surgery), the patient had a post-operative oncology visit. Performance status ECOG 1. Wound healing well. CA-125 post-operatively (4 weeks): 847 U/mL (down from 4,820 U/mL at diagnosis). Discussed adjuvant chemotherapy plan: carboplatin AUC 5 / paclitaxel 175 mg/m2 IV every 21 days for 6 cycles. Discussed addition of bevacizumab; patient declined due to concerns about delayed wound healing given recent bowel resection and per protocol delay recommendation. Germline BRCA1 result reviewed; patient referred to genetic counseling for cascade testing of children. Discussed PARP inhibitor maintenance therapy after chemotherapy completion. Patient agreeable to plan. Laboratory values: WBC 7.2 K/uL, Hgb 9.8 g/dL (mild anemia, transfusion not required), Plt 312 K/uL, Cr 0.82 mg/dL, AST 28 U/L, ALT 31 U/L, ALP 88 U/L, Total Bili 0.6 mg/dL. Neuropathy baseline assessment: none.

At age 58 years, the patient received cycle 1 of curative-intent adjuvant carboplatin AUC 5 / paclitaxel 175 mg/m2 IV every 21 days. Premedication: dexamethasone 20 mg IV, diphenhydramine 25 mg IV, famotidine 20 mg IV, ondansetron 8 mg IV. Carboplatin dose: 635 mg IV over 30 minutes. Paclitaxel dose: 289 mg IV over 3 hours. Tolerated well. No hypersensitivity reaction. Post-cycle antiemetics: ondansetron 8 mg orally TID x3 days, dexamethasone 4 mg BID x2 days, prochlorperazine PRN.

At age 58 years, after cycle 1 of carboplatin/paclitaxel, the patient developed ALOPECIA (CTCAE Grade 2), FATIGUE (CTCAE Grade 2), and NAUSEA (CTCAE Grade 1, well controlled with antiemetics). Alopecia was expected and scalp cooling was not used per patient preference. Fatigue was managed with activity pacing. No significant hematologic toxicity at day 14 nadir: WBC 3.1 K/uL, ANC 1.4 K/uL, Hgb 9.2 g/dL, Plt 198 K/uL.

At age 58 years, the patient received cycles 2, 3, and 4 of carboplatin AUC 5 / paclitaxel 175 mg/m2 IV every 21 days without dose modification. All cycles administered on schedule. CA-125 after cycle 3: 42 U/mL (normal range <35 U/mL; near normalization). Cumulative paclitaxel-related peripheral neuropathy developing: Grade 1 sensory neuropathy in bilateral fingertips and toes noted at cycle 4 evaluation. CBC at cycle 4 day 1: WBC 5.4 K/uL, ANC 3.2 K/uL, Hgb 10.1 g/dL, Plt 245 K/uL.

At age 58 years (after cycle 3 of carboplatin/paclitaxel), the patient had a CT scan of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis with contrast (interim restaging), which showed no cancer (complete response). No evidence of residual or recurrent peritoneal disease. No ascites. No pleural effusion. Lymph nodes within normal limits. Post-surgical changes noted in pelvis and right upper quadrant consistent with prior debulking surgery and cholecystectomy/splenectomy. No hepatic parenchymal metastases. No pulmonary nodules. Impression: Complete radiographic response to adjuvant chemotherapy following primary debulking surgery.

At age 58 years, the patient received cycles 5 and 6 of carboplatin AUC 5 / paclitaxel 175 mg/m2 IV every 21 days, completing the planned 6-cycle adjuvant course. Paclitaxel dose was reduced to 155 mg/m2 (89% of planned dose) for cycle 6 due to worsening Grade 2 sensory peripheral neuropathy affecting bilateral hands and feet, with difficulty with fine motor tasks. Cycle 6 completed without further complications. CA-125 after cycle 6: 18 U/mL (normal). End-of-treatment CBC: WBC 5.8 K/uL, Hgb 10.6 g/dL, Plt 262 K/uL.

At age 58 years, at completion of 6 cycles of carboplatin/paclitaxel, the patient had cumulative PERIPHERAL NEUROPATHY (CTCAE Grade 2, sensory) affecting bilateral hands and feet, with numbness and tingling interfering with fine motor tasks but not activities of daily living. Referred to physical therapy and occupational therapy for neuropathy management. Started on duloxetine 30 mg daily titrated to 60 mg daily for neuropathic pain and functional improvement. Partial improvement over subsequent 3 months of follow-up (Grade 1 residual neuropathy).

At age 58 years (post-chemotherapy, pre-maintenance assessment), the patient had a gynecologic oncology visit to discuss maintenance therapy. CA-125: 18 U/mL (normal). CT restaging post-cycle 6: complete radiographic response confirmed. ECOG PS: 1. Peripheral neuropathy: Grade 1 (improved from Grade 2). Discussed options for maintenance therapy: niraparib (FDA approved for all-comers post first-line platinum-based chemotherapy, regardless of BRCA status; particularly indicated for BRCA-mutated patients). Olaparib also discussed as an option; patient chose niraparib. Baseline complete blood count and platelets reviewed prior to initiating niraparib. Baseline weight: 62 kg. Baseline platelet count: 272 K/uL. Per PRIMA trial individualized dosing: Given weight <77 kg and platelets <150 K/uL criterion not applicable, standard starting dose 200 mg daily discussed; however, given weight of 62 kg, weight-based dosing recommended at 200 mg daily (some institutional protocols use 200 mg for weight <77 kg). Patient counseled on hematologic monitoring, fatigue, nausea, hypertension. Agreed to proceed.

At age 58 years (approximately 7 weeks after completing cycle 6 of carboplatin/paclitaxel), the patient was initiated on niraparib 200 mg orally once daily as maintenance therapy. Counseled to take at bedtime to minimize nausea. Antiemetic: ondansetron 4 mg PRN for nausea. Antihypertensive: lisinopril continued; blood pressure monitoring at home twice weekly given niraparib-associated hypertension risk. CBC monitoring: weekly for first 4 weeks, then monthly thereafter.

At age 58 years, approximately 3 weeks after initiating niraparib 200 mg daily, the patient developed THROMBOCYTOPENIA (CTCAE Grade 3) with platelet count of 42 K/uL on weekly CBC monitoring (down from baseline 272 K/uL). Patient had no active bleeding symptoms. No petechiae or purpura on exam. Niraparib was held per protocol. Bone marrow suppression versus immune thrombocytopenia (ITP) considered. Peripheral smear reviewed: no schistocytes (excluding TTP/HUS), large platelets consistent with peripheral destruction versus marrow suppression. Niraparib-induced thrombocytopenia considered most likely. Niraparib held for 14 days. Repeat CBC at day 7 of hold: platelets 98 K/uL. Repeat CBC at day 14 of hold: platelets 167 K/uL (recovery to Grade 0). Niraparib resumed at reduced dose of 100 mg daily per package insert recommendations for Grade 3/4 thrombocytopenia.

At age 58 years, approximately 6 weeks after resuming niraparib at 100 mg daily, the patient developed recurrent THROMBOCYTOPENIA (CTCAE Grade 2) with platelet count of 68 K/uL. No bleeding symptoms. Niraparib held again for 7 days. Platelet recovery to 145 K/uL. Hematology consultation obtained. Bone marrow biopsy performed to evaluate for marrow infiltration or aplasia: hypocellular marrow (30% cellularity for age) with megakaryocyte hypoplasia, consistent with drug-induced myelosuppression; no evidence of malignant infiltration. Niraparib resumed at 100 mg daily (no further dose reduction available per label; discuss risk/benefit of continuation).

At age 59 years (approximately 9 months after completing primary chemotherapy, 6 months on niraparib maintenance), the patient had a CT scan of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis with contrast (surveillance restaging), which showed no cancer. No evidence of peritoneal carcinomatosis recurrence. No ascites. No adenopathy. No parenchymal metastases. Post-surgical changes stable. CA-125 at this timepoint: 21 U/mL (normal). Impression: Continued complete radiographic response. No evidence of disease recurrence.

At age 59 years (on niraparib 100 mg daily, approximately 8 months of maintenance), the patient developed HYPERTENSION (CTCAE Grade 2) with blood pressure readings of 158-164/92-98 mmHg on home monitoring. Lisinopril dose increased from 10 mg to 20 mg daily by primary care physician in coordination with oncology. Amlodipine 5 mg daily added. Blood pressure improved to 132-138/82-86 mmHg over subsequent 4 weeks. Niraparib continued without dose modification. Additional complaint: FATIGUE (CTCAE Grade 1-2, ongoing), managed with activity modification and support.

At age 59 years (12 months of niraparib maintenance), the patient had a routine surveillance oncology visit. CA-125: 19 U/mL (normal, stable). CBC: WBC 5.2 K/uL, Hgb 11.0 g/dL, Plt 132 K/uL (Grade 1 thrombocytopenia, stable). Blood pressure: 134/84 mmHg (improved, Grade 1). Peripheral neuropathy: Grade 1 (stable residual). ECOG PS: 1. Patient tolerating niraparib 100 mg daily with manageable toxicities. Discussed ongoing benefit of PARP inhibitor maintenance in BRCA1-mutated HGSOC (PRIMA, SOLO-1 data reviewed). Patient wishes to continue. Quality of life assessment: moderate; fatigue remains most burdensome symptom. Counseled on importance of continuing antihypertensive medications. Next imaging in 6 months. Genetic counseling completed; two adult daughters undergoing cascade BRCA1 testing.

At age 59 years (approximately 18 months after completing primary chemotherapy, 15 months on niraparib maintenance), the patient had a CT scan of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis with contrast (surveillance restaging), which showed no cancer. No evidence of disease recurrence. No new lymphadenopathy, ascites, or peritoneal implants. CA-125: 22 U/mL (normal). Impression: Durable complete remission maintained on niraparib maintenance. No evidence of disease.

At age 59 years (18 months of niraparib maintenance), the patient had an oncology surveillance visit. CA-125: 22 U/mL (normal, stable). CBC: WBC 5.6 K/uL, Hgb 11.3 g/dL, Plt 148 K/uL (Grade 1 thrombocytopenia, stable, chronically). Blood pressure well controlled on dual antihypertensive therapy. ECOG PS: 1. Patient continues niraparib 100 mg daily. Peripheral neuropathy remains Grade 1 (stable). Discussion: Based on current evidence and SOLO-1 trial data showing benefit up to 5 years of maintenance in BRCA-mutated HGSOC, plan to continue niraparib indefinitely until disease progression, unacceptable toxicity, or completion of planned duration. Thrombocytopenia chronically at Grade 1 level; acceptable for continuation. Quality of life: improved from earlier in maintenance course; patient has returned to part-time work as a secondary school teacher. Genetic counseling update: one daughter tested BRCA1 positive and referred to high-risk breast/ovarian cancer surveillance program.

At age 60 years (approximately 27 months of niraparib maintenance, 30 months after primary debulking surgery), the patient had a CT scan of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis with contrast (surveillance restaging), which showed no cancer. Continued complete radiographic remission. No peritoneal, lymph node, or parenchymal metastases. No ascites. CA-125: 24 U/mL (normal). Impression: Sustained complete remission on niraparib maintenance therapy.

At age 60 years (approximately 27 months of niraparib maintenance), the patient had a routine surveillance visit. CA-125: 24 U/mL (normal, trend stable). CBC: WBC 5.9 K/uL, Hgb 11.6 g/dL, Plt 155 K/uL (Grade 1 thrombocytopenia, stable chronic). Blood pressure: 128/80 mmHg (well controlled). ECOG PS: 1. Peripheral neuropathy: Grade 1 (stable). No new symptoms. Tolerating niraparib 100 mg daily. Patient in sustained complete remission now approximately 2.5 years from diagnosis. Discussion: Continued niraparib maintenance recommended. Annual surveillance CT to continue. Discussed clinical trial options for next-line therapy should relapse occur. Patient educated on signs and symptoms of recurrence (new abdominal symptoms, increased abdominal girth, new pelvic pain). Overall status: patient is in complete remission, tolerating maintenance PARP inhibitor with manageable chronic toxicities, with excellent disease control reflecting the clinical benefit of PARP inhibition in germline BRCA1-mutated high-grade serous ovarian cancer.

documents.text:
CT CHEST, ABDOMEN AND PELVIS WITH CONTRAST
INTERIM RESTAGING EXAMINATION

CLINICAL INDICATION: 58-year-old female with known high-grade serous ovarian carcinoma, FIGO stage IIIC. Status post primary debulking surgery including TAH-BSO, omentectomy, pelvic and para-aortic lymph node dissection, rectosigmoid resection, and splenectomy. Now on adjuvant carboplatin/paclitaxel chemotherapy; this examination obtained after completion of cycle 3 for interim restaging.

COMPARISON: Prior staging CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis (pre-operative).

TECHNIQUE: Multidetector CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis was performed following the administration of intravenous iodinated contrast (100 mL Omnipaque 350) in the portal venous phase. Oral contrast was also administered. Coronal and sagittal reformats were reviewed.

FINDINGS:

CHEST:
Lungs: The lungs are clear. No pulmonary nodules identified. No consolidation, ground-glass opacity, or pleural-based masses. The previously noted small bilateral pleural effusions have resolved. The airways are patent to the subsegmental level.
Pleura: No pleural effusion. No pneumothorax.
Mediastinum and hila: Heart size is normal. No pericardial effusion. No mediastinal or hilar lymphadenopathy. The thoracic aorta is normal in caliber. Thyroid not fully imaged.
Chest wall and bones: No suspicious osseous lesion identified in the imaged thorax.

ABDOMEN:
Liver: The liver is normal in size and attenuation. No focal hepatic lesion identified. The previously documented periportal lymphadenopathy has resolved. No biliary ductal dilation. Post-surgical changes are noted in the right upper quadrant consistent with prior cholecystectomy — surgical clips are seen in the gallbladder fossa; no gallbladder is identified, as expected.
Spleen: The spleen is absent consistent with prior splenectomy. The splenectomy bed demonstrates expected postoperative fibrofatty changes without discrete fluid collection or mass.
Pancreas: The pancreas appears unremarkable. No ductal dilation. No peripancreatic fat stranding.
Kidneys and adrenal glands: The kidneys are symmetric in size and show normal enhancement. No hydronephrosis. No renal masses. The adrenal glands are unremarkable.
Stomach and bowel: Postsurgical changes are present in keeping with prior rectosigmoid resection; surgical staple line is identified in the pelvis with no anastomotic leak or peri-anastomotic fluid collection evident. The remainder of the visualized small and large bowel appears within normal limits. No bowel obstruction or wall thickening.
Peritoneum and omentum: The omentum is absent consistent with prior omentectomy. No peritoneal soft tissue nodularity or enhancement is identified. No ascites. The mesenteric fat planes are clean without discrete nodal masses or implants. No serosal deposits seen along the right or left hemidiaphragm, paracolic gutters, or bowel surfaces.
Retroperitoneum: No retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy. The aorta and IVC are normal. No retroperitoneal mass or fluid collection.

PELVIS:
Uterus and adnexa: The uterus and bilateral adnexa are absent consistent with prior total hysterectomy and bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy. The pelvic surgical bed demonstrates expected postoperative changes including a small amount of pelvic fibrofatty soft tissue and surgical clips, without evidence of local recurrence or discrete mass.
Bladder: The urinary bladder appears unremarkable. No wall thickening or intraluminal filling defect.
Pelvic lymph nodes: No pelvic sidewall or inguinal lymphadenopathy. The external and internal iliac nodal chains are unremarkable.
Pelvic floor: No pelvic free fluid. No pelvic mass.

BONES: No suspicious lytic or blastic osseous lesion identified throughout the imaged skeleton.

SOFT TISSUES: No subcutaneous or soft tissue masses. Small subcutaneous seroma/fluid collection noted along the lower midline abdominal wall incision site, measuring approximately 2.3 cm in greatest dimension — this is felt to represent a postoperative seroma and appears stable in appearance; no surrounding cellulitis or drainable fluid collection.

IMPRESSION:
1. No evidence of residual or recurrent peritoneal carcinomatosis. Resolution of the previously documented omental cake, peritoneal implants, and ascites.
2. No new hepatic, pulmonary, or distant metastatic disease identified.
3. Lymph nodes are within normal limits throughout the chest, abdomen, and pelvis.
4. Expected postoperative changes related to prior debulking surgery, including absence of spleen, gallbladder, uterus, and bilateral adnexa; rectosigmoid anastomosis appears intact.
5. Small lower midline abdominal wall seroma, likely related to prior surgery — clinically correlate.
6. Overall, a markedly improved examination compared to the pre-operative staging study, with no detectable residual disease.

GYNECOLOGIC ONCOLOGY CLINIC — FOLLOW-UP NOTE

ATTENDING: [Attending GynOnc]
FELLOW/RESIDENT: [Fellow]
VISIT TYPE: Follow-up / Maintenance Therapy Planning

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT:
Ms. Okonkwo is a 58-year-old postmenopausal West African woman with FIGO stage IIIC high-grade serous ovarian carcinoma (HGSOC), germline BRCA1-positive (c.5266dupC, frameshift), presenting today for post-chemotherapy restaging assessment and discussion of maintenance therapy options after completing 6 cycles of first-line carboplatin/paclitaxel.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:
Ms. Okonkwo was originally diagnosed with FIGO IIIC HGSOC following work-up for increasing abdominal distension and bloating. Staging CT at diagnosis demonstrated advanced ovarian/peritoneal disease. She underwent omental biopsy confirming HGSOC. Germline testing (sent at time of diagnosis) returned positive for BRCA1 c.5266dupC (also known as 5382insC), a well-characterized pathogenic frameshift variant; tumor also demonstrated HRD positivity and CCNE1 amplification on NGS profiling.

She underwent primary debulking surgery (PDS) with R0 resection (no gross residual disease). Surgical pathology confirmed HGSOC with STIC (serous tubal intraepithelial carcinoma), lymph node involvement — staged pT3c pN1. Post-operative course was complicated by ileus and a wound seroma, both managed conservatively.

She commenced adjuvant carboplatin (AUC 5) / paclitaxel (Taxol, 175 mg/m2) q21 days. Pre-cycle 4 CA-125 was 42 U/mL (down from 847 post-op), and mid-treatment CT after cycle 3 showed an excellent response with no measurable residual disease. She developed Grade 1 peripheral neuropathy early on and cumulative Grade 2 sensory peripheral neuropathy by cycle 6 (primarily fingertips and feet, no functional impairment). Paclitaxel was dose-reduced by 20% for cycle 6. She was started on duloxetine (Cymbalta) 30 mg daily for neuropathy symptom management, with some reported improvement. Alopecia, nausea, and fatigue were noted throughout — all manageable, no dose holds otherwise.

Today she presents having completed C6 approximately 3.5 weeks ago. Post-C6 CA-125 returned at 18 U/mL (institution normal <35). Restaging CT chest/abdomen/pelvis was performed and reviewed (see below). She comes today to review results and plan next steps, specifically maintenance therapy.

She reports feeling "much better" over the past 2 weeks — energy gradually improving. Neuropathy still present but she rates it a 2-3/10, improved from peak of 5-6/10 during chemo. She is tolerating duloxetine without issue. No new symptoms. She is not currently on any other cancer-directed therapy.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS:
- Constitutional: Fatigue improving week over week. No fevers, no chills, no night sweats.
- Neurological: Persistent mild bilateral distal sensory changes in fingers and feet. No gait instability. No new deficits.
- GI: Appetite much improved. No nausea currently. Normal bowel function. No abdominal pain.
- GU: No dysuria, hematuria. No vaginal bleeding.
- Cardiovascular: Known hypertension, reports BP running around 130s/80s on lisinopril. No chest pain, no palpitations, no edema.
- Hematologic: No unusual bruising or bleeding noted.
- MSK: Mild arthralgias, improving.
- All other systems reviewed and negative.

---

PAST MEDICAL HISTORY:
1. FIGO IIIC HGSOC, BRCA1 germline positive — as above
2. Hypertension
3. Remote appendectomy

FAMILY HISTORY:
- Mother: breast cancer, diagnosed age 45
- Maternal aunt: ovarian cancer
(Consistent with BRCA1 hereditary breast-ovarian cancer syndrome)

SOCIAL HISTORY:
Non-smoker. Occasional alcohol (1-2 drinks/week). Retired schoolteacher. Lives with spouse. Two adult children; one daughter has been counseled regarding cascade genetic testing. Good social support. Does not use recreational substances.

MEDICATIONS:
1. Lisinopril 10 mg daily (HTN)
2. Duloxetine (Cymbalta) 30 mg daily (neuropathy)
3. Ondansetron 4 mg PRN (residual script, minimal use now)
4. Loratadine PRN
5. Vitamin D 1000 IU daily

ALLERGIES: NKDA

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION:
Vitals: BP 132/78, HR 76, RR 16, SpO2 98% RA, Temp 36.8°C, Weight 62 kg, Height 163 cm, BSA ~1.66 m2
ECOG Performance Status: 1

General: Well-appearing, well-nourished woman in no acute distress. Alert and conversational.
HEENT: Alopecia — scalp with early regrowth noted, ~0.5 cm. No lymphadenopathy in cervical or supraclavicular regions.
Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm, no murmurs.
Respiratory: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No pleural rub.
Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, non-distended. Midline and Pfannenstiel incision scars well-healed. No palpable masses. No ascites appreciated on exam.
Pelvic: Deferred per patient preference (done at last visit, no current indication to repeat).
Extremities: No lower extremity edema. Peripheral pulses intact.
Neurological: Intact cranial nerves. Reduced pinprick and light touch sensation bilateral distal fingers and toes (stocking-glove pattern), consistent with chemotherapy-induced peripheral neuropathy (CIPN) Grade 1-2. Gait steady.

---

LAB RESULTS (drawn today, unless noted):
- CA-125: 18 U/mL (normal range <35) — normalized
- CBC: WBC 5.8, Hgb 11.6, Plt 272 K/uL
- CMP: BUN 14, Cr 0.82 (eGFR >60), LFTs WNL, electrolytes WNL
- Note: CBC obtained today in preparation for potential maintenance therapy initiation

---

IMAGING:
Restaging CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast, performed approximately 3 weeks post-C6: No evidence of residual or recurrent disease. No ascites. No pleural effusion. Peritoneal surfaces appear clear. Previously noted omental disease is not visualized. Retroperitoneal and pelvic lymph nodes within normal limits. Solid organs unremarkable. Overall findings consistent with complete radiographic response.

---

ASSESSMENT:
1. FIGO IIIC HGSOC, germline BRCA1 positive (c.5266dupC), HRD+, status post R0 PDS and 6 cycles carboplatin/paclitaxel — complete response by imaging and CA-125 normalization (18 U/mL).
2. Chemotherapy-induced peripheral neuropathy, Grade 1 (improved from Grade 2 at end of treatment), on duloxetine with partial symptom benefit.
3. Hypertension, controlled on lisinopril.

---

PLAN:

1. MAINTENANCE THERAPY — DISCUSSION:

Ms. Okonkwo has achieved a complete response to first-line platinum-based doublet chemotherapy and is an excellent candidate for PARP inhibitor maintenance. Her germline BRCA1 mutation is the strongest single predictor of benefit from this class of agent. We discussed the following options at length:

a) NIRAPARIB (Zejula): FDA-approved for maintenance therapy in all women with advanced ovarian cancer in complete or partial response after first-line platinum-based chemo, regardless of BRCA or HRD status (based on PRIMA trial). For BRCA-mutated patients, the PFS benefit is particularly marked (HR ~0.40 in the PRIMA BRCAmut cohort). PRIMA also established individualized starting dosing (ISD): patients with body weight <77 kg OR platelet count <150 K/uL start at 200 mg daily rather than 300 mg. Ms. Okonkwo weighs 62 kg — she falls in the weight-based reduced starting dose category and would begin at 200 mg daily. Her platelets today are 272 K/uL, so the platelet criterion for reduction does not apply, but the weight criterion alone is sufficient. Dosing to be 200 mg PO daily.

Side effects discussed: myelosuppression (especially thrombocytopenia and anemia — most common in first 3 months, requires close CBC monitoring), nausea, fatigue, hypertension, and the rare but reported risk of MDS/AML with prolonged PARP inhibitor use (low absolute risk, particularly in first 2 years). We emphasized the importance of CBC checks at weeks 1, 2, 4, then monthly for the first year, with dose holds and reductions per protocol for significant cytopenias.

b) OLAPARIB (Lynparza): Also FDA-approved as maintenance for patients with germline BRCA1/2 mutations in complete/partial response after first-line platinum (SOLO-1 trial; 7-year follow-up data now available showing durable benefit). Standard dose 300 mg PO BID. Similar class effects — nausea, fatigue, anemia, and hematologic monitoring required. SOLO-1 restricted to BRCA-mutated patients; does not have the broader indication of niraparib. QD vs BID dosing is one practical distinction.

Patient was counseled that either agent is reasonable and well-supported for her scenario. She is aware of the neuropathy concern — both agents can cause fatigue; neither is specifically neurotoxic. We noted that given her BRCA1 status, she has the most data from SOLO-1 with olaparib, but PRIMA data for niraparib in BRCA-mutated patients is also compelling. After discussion, she expressed preference for once-daily dosing and chose niraparib.

We reviewed baseline CBC today — Plt 272, Hgb 11.6. She is not thrombocytopenic at baseline. Will initiate niraparib 200 mg PO daily (weight-based ISD, 62 kg) after today's visit. Rx sent to specialty pharmacy.

Monitoring plan: CBC at 2 weeks, 4 weeks, then monthly x 12 months, then q3 months. She has my number and nursing triage contact for any unexpected bruising, petechiae, or unusual fatigue/shortness of breath. Nausea management: take with food, consider low-dose prochlorperazine PRN if needed.

2. NEUROPATHY: Continue duloxetine 30 mg daily. Will reassess at next visit. Expected slow improvement over months. She should avoid activities with fall risk if numbness worsens.

3. HYPERTENSION: Continue lisinopril. Will monitor BP at each visit; niraparib is associated with hypertension and she already has a baseline diagnosis — may need uptitration.

4. BRCA1 HEREDITARY CANCER SYNDROME: Counseled regarding her daughter's eligibility for cascade testing through the genetic counseling program. Referral placed previously; recommend follow-up.

5. RETURN TO CLINIC: In 4 weeks for CBC check and niraparib tolerance assessment. Full follow-up visit with imaging in approximately 3 months.

Questions answered. Patient verbalized understanding. Informed consent obtained. Patient agrees to proceed with plan.

TYPE OF PROCEDURE
Image-guided percutaneous core needle biopsy of omental mass (CT-guided; 18-gauge core needle, 4 cores submitted); concurrent diagnostic paracentesis with peritoneal washing submitted for cytologic evaluation.

------------------------------------------------------------------------
GROSS DESCRIPTION

SP-A — OMENTAL CORE BIOPSY:
Received in formalin, labeled "omental core biopsy," are four (4) tan-white to pale pink, firm cylindrical tissue cores ranging from 1.1 to 1.6 cm in length and approximately 0.1 cm in diameter. The cores are grossly firm and homogeneous with focal areas of chalky white discoloration, raising the possibility of necrosis or calcification. All four cores are entirely submitted in a single cassette (A1).

SP-B — PERITONEAL WASHING:
Received is a 50 mL aliquot of straw-colored to mildly turbid fluid labeled "peritoneal washing." The fluid is mildly viscous and slightly hemorrhagic. Specimen processed by ThinPrep method with concurrent Papanicolaou stain and cell block preparation. Cell block submitted in cassette (B1).

------------------------------------------------------------------------
MICROSCOPIC DESCRIPTION AND DIAGNOSTIC FINDINGS

SP-A — OMENTAL CORE BIOPSY:
The cores show complete replacement of the normal adipose and fibrovascular stroma of the omentum by a poorly differentiated carcinoma arranged in solid sheets, trabeculae, and poorly formed nests. The neoplastic cells are large and polygonal with abundant pale to eosinophilic cytoplasm. Nuclear features are markedly atypical: nuclei are enlarged, vesicular to hyperchromatic, with conspicuous macronucleoli (many cells with 2-3 nucleoli). Nuclear contour irregularity is marked. Mitotic figures are numerous, exceeding 10 per high-power field (HPF), and atypical mitoses are readily identified.

Psammoma bodies are present, scattered throughout the tumor and focally clustered. These laminated calcific concentric structures are a characteristic and diagnostically relevant finding. A desmoplastic stromal response is noted at the periphery of tumor nests. There is focal tumor necrosis (comedo-type). No lymphovascular space invasion is identified in this limited biopsy material, though assessment is constrained by specimen size. No normal ovarian or tubal tissue is sampled in these cores.

SP-B — PERITONEAL WASHING / CYTOLOGY:
The ThinPrep preparation demonstrates a markedly cellular specimen with numerous three-dimensional papillary clusters and sheets of highly atypical epithelial cells, many with the characteristic "cannonball" architecture. Individual cells show enlarged nuclei, prominent nucleoli, and high nuclear-to-cytoplasmic ratios. Macrophages and reactive mesothelial cells are present in the background. Psammoma bodies are also identified within cell clusters on the ThinPrep preparation. The cell block preparation confirms the above findings, with cohesive clusters of high-grade epithelial cells with marked nuclear pleomorphism embedded in a fibrinous background.

COMMENTS

1. MORPHOLOGIC AND IMMUNOPHENOTYPIC PROFILE: The combination of high-grade nuclear features, psammoma bodies, diffuse PAX8 and WT-1 positivity, aberrant p53 (overexpression pattern), and CK7+/CK20- profile is characteristic of high-grade serous carcinoma (HGSOC) of Mullerian origin. This immunoprofile strongly supports a primary ovarian, tubal, or peritoneal origin rather than a metastasis from the gastrointestinal tract, lung, or other site. NAPSIN-A and TTF-1 negativity argues against pulmonary adenocarcinoma. CK20 negativity argues against colorectal primary.

2. p53 ABERRANT PATTERN: The diffuse block-like overexpression of p53 (>90% of cells, strong intensity) is consistent with a missense gain-of-function mutation in TP53, a hallmark molecular alteration in HGSOC occurring in >95% of cases. This pattern is distinct from the null/complete absence pattern seen with nonsense or frameshift mutations and from wild-type focal weak expression.

3. ER POSITIVITY: Focal estrogen receptor positivity (30%) is not uncommon in HGSOC and does not alter the histologic classification. It should not be interpreted as indicative of an endometrioid or low-grade serous histotype in the context of the overall morphologic and immunophenotypic picture.

4. SITE OF ORIGIN: Primary ovarian versus primary peritoneal serous carcinoma cannot be definitively distinguished on biopsy material alone, as no ovarian or tubal tissue is present in the submitted cores. Definitive site assignment will require surgical resection specimen evaluation per the 2020 WHO Classification and SGO/ISGYP criteria (including assessment for serous tubal intraepithelial carcinoma, STIC).

5. PSAMMOMA BODIES: The presence of psammoma bodies in both the core biopsy and peritoneal washing cytology supports a serous histotype and is consistent with the clinical and radiographic impression.

6. GRADING: Tumor is classified as high-grade (Grade 3) by architectural and cytologic criteria. By the two-tier system (low-grade vs. high-grade serous carcinoma), this is unequivocally high-grade based on nuclear grade (marked pleomorphism, prominent nucleoli) and mitotic rate (>10/HPF). The two-tier designation supersedes the older three-tier FIGO grading in current practice for serous carcinomas.

7. Ki-67 80%: Proliferative index is markedly elevated, consistent with the high-grade designation.

METHODOLOGY
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Tumor Sequencing: Hybrid-capture-based next-generation sequencing of 648-gene
panel covering all coding exons of clinically actionable oncogenes and tumor
suppressor genes; mean coverage depth >500x. Copy number variation (CNV)
analysis by normalized read-depth ratio. Structural variant (SV) analysis by
discordant read-pair and split-read methods. Genomic LOH calculated across the
autosomes using SNP-based allelic imbalance quantification. HRD composite score
calculated incorporating genomic LOH, telomeric allelic imbalance (TAI), and
large-scale state transitions (LST) (Myriad/SBS-based algorithm, threshold >42).
TMB calculated from synonymous and non-synonymous somatic point mutations across
covered genomic territory (~1.1 Mb). MSI status assessed by analysis of 95
homopolymer and microsatellite loci.

Germline Testing: Concurrent peripheral blood germline sequencing of 84-gene
hereditary cancer panel (includes BRCA1, BRCA2, PALB2, RAD51C, RAD51D, ATM,
BRIP1, MLH1, MSH2, MSH6, PMS2, TP53, PTEN, STK11, CDH1, and others). Sanger
confirmation performed for all pathogenic or likely pathogenic germline variants.

Variant Classification: Variants classified per ACMG/AMP 2015 guidelines.
Somatic variants reported at VAF >=5% in tumor (lower thresholds applied for
targeted hotspots). Germline variants classified as Pathogenic (P), Likely
Pathogenic (LP), Variant of Uncertain Significance (VUS), Likely Benign, or
Benign. Actionability tiered per OncoKB/AMP-ASCO-CAP joint consensus framework
(Tier I: strong clinical evidence; Tier II: potential clinical significance;
Tier III: unknown significance; Tier IV: benign/likely benign).

Interpretation:
A well-characterized pathogenic frameshift mutation in BRCA1 exon 20 was
identified in peripheral blood, confirming germline origin. This variant results
in premature truncation at position 1756+74, leading to loss of the BRCT domain
critical for DNA double-strand break repair via the homologous recombination
pathway. The c.5266dupC (5382insC) variant is among the most prevalent BRCA1
founders mutations worldwide, with high prevalence in Ashkenazi Jewish, Polish,
Belarus, and certain sub-Saharan West African populations. This variant
constitutes a Tier IA actionable alteration (strong clinical evidence for
therapeutic benefit from PARP inhibitor therapy in ovarian cancer). This finding
has implications for hereditary breast and ovarian cancer (HBOC) syndrome and
should prompt genetic counseling referral for the patient and first-degree
relatives.

variants identified in BRCA2, PALB2, RAD51C, RAD51D, ATM, BRIP1, MLH1, MSH2,

TP53 p.R175H Notes: The R175H hotspot is the most common TP53 gain-of-function
missense mutation in human cancer. VAF of 68% is consistent with near-clonal
alteration in a tumor of estimated 65% purity, suggesting this is a truncal
event. This is an expected and almost ubiquitous finding in HGSOC (>95% of

by IHC was noted on the concurrent pathology report, consistent with this
missense variant.

BRCA1 LOH Notes: The germline BRCA1 pathogenic allele is present in tumor; the
wild-type allele demonstrates somatic loss of heterozygosity (LOH) as
demonstrated by allele-specific copy number analysis. Biallelic inactivation of
BRCA1 (germline pathogenic frameshift + somatic LOH of the remaining wild-type
allele) confirms functionally deficient BRCA1 in the tumor, consistent with HRD-
positive phenotype and predicted PARP inhibitor sensitivity.

accession site; high prior probability of aberrant splicing resulting in exon
skipping and loss of function. Subclonal VAF of 22% may reflect clonal
heterogeneity. RB1 alterations are identified in a subset of HGSOC and may be
associated with chemotherapy resistance through mechanisms distinct from HRD;
clinical significance in this molecular context is uncertain.

NF1 Notes: Frameshift variant in exon 35 at subclonal VAF of 19% consistent with
subclonal RAS-pathway deregulation. NF1 loss activates RAS/MAPK signaling but
has not been validated as a predictive or prognostic biomarker in HGSOC
specifically.

Whole-arm / broad somatic CNV landscape (reported for context):
- 1q gain, 3q gain, 8q gain: consistent with HGSOC genomic background
- 4q loss, 5q loss, 13q loss (includes RB1 locus), 17p loss, 17q loss
  (BRCA1 locus concordant with LOH), 22q loss: consistent with HGSOC
- Multiple chromosomal regions of LOH identified (elevated genomic complexity)

CCNE1 Amplification Notes: CCNE1 (cyclin E1) amplification at copy number 8.4
represents high-level amplification of the 19q12 locus. CCNE1 amplification
occurs in approximately 15-20% of HGSOC and promotes G1/S cell cycle progression
independent of CDK4/6-Rb regulation. CCNE1 amplification has been associated in
some retrospective analyses with relative primary platinum resistance and
diminished response to PARP inhibitors via restoration of replication fork
progression; however, the prognostic and predictive significance of CCNE1
amplification in the specific context of germline BRCA1-mutated HGSOC is
uncertain, and most patients with this combination remain eligible for and may
benefit from PARP inhibitor-based therapy. This finding is noted for clinical
consideration and does not currently exclude PARP inhibitor use.

MYC gain at copy number 3.8 is low-level and of uncertain clinical significance.

HRD Score Interpretation: An HRD composite score of 48 is above the validated
positivity threshold of >42 (myChoice HRD-equivalent algorithm). This reflects
widespread genomic scarring attributable to defects in the homologous
recombination repair pathway, consistent with functional loss of BRCA1. HRD-
positive status, in combination with BRCA1 biallelic inactivation (germline
pathogenic + somatic LOH), supports anticipated sensitivity to platinum-based
chemotherapy and PARP inhibitor-based maintenance therapy. HRD-positive tumors
have been shown to derive significant benefit from PARP inhibitor maintenance
following platinum-based chemotherapy in the first-line setting.

TMB and MSI: TMB of 4.2 mut/Mb is low; MSS status confirmed. These findings are
typical for HGSOC, which is characterized by a low overall somatic mutation
burden despite the presence of copy number instability and HRD. Immunotherapy
with checkpoint inhibitors targeting PD-1/PD-L1 has demonstrated limited
efficacy as a single agent in unselected HGSOC; the low TMB and MSS status in
this tumor are consistent with expected biology and do not provide a basis for
single-agent immune checkpoint inhibitor selection at this time.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
ACTIONABILITY SUMMARY
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Tier IA -- Strong Clinical Evidence (FDA-Approved Indication):
  - BRCA1 germline pathogenic variant (c.5266dupC): PARP inhibitor sensitivity.
    FDA-approved PARP inhibitors with indications in germline BRCA1/2-mutated
    ovarian cancer (first-line maintenance after platinum-based chemotherapy):
    olaparib (Lynparza), niraparib (Zejula), rucaparib (Rubraca -- note
    regulatory status subject to change). These agents exploit synthetic
    lethality in BRCA1-deficient tumor cells.

Tier IB -- Strong Clinical Evidence (Regulatory or Guideline Support):
  - HRD-positive status (score 48, with BRCA1 biallelic inactivation): PARP
    inhibitor benefit in first-line maintenance setting (e.g., olaparib +
    bevacizumab per PAOLA-1 data; niraparib per PRIMA/ENGOT-OV26 HRD-positive
    subgroup data). HRD-positive status without BRCA mutation (HRD+/BRCAwt) also
    supports PARP inhibitor maintenance; in BRCA-mutated context, PARP inhibitor
    benefit is particularly pronounced.

Tier II -- Investigational / Potential Clinical Significance:
  - CCNE1 high-level amplification: Emerging data suggest possible reduced
    benefit from PARP inhibitor monotherapy in some CCNE1-amplified tumors;
    however, this has not been validated in the context of germline BRCA1
    mutation with biallelic LOH. Not currently actionable as a contraindication
    to standard therapy. Clinical trials investigating CDK2 inhibitors and
    WEE1/PKMYT1 inhibitors in CCNE1-amplified ovarian cancer may be relevant if
    resistance develops.
  - RB1 splice site mutation: Subclonal, uncertain prognostic significance in
    HGSOC. May contribute to chemotherapy resistance if dominant clones emerge.

Tier III -- Unknown Significance:
  - NF1 p.Q1966fs (subclonal)
  - MYC copy number gain
  - RB1 c.1386+1G>A (subclonal context adds uncertainty)

GENETIC COUNSELING RECOMMENDATION
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Identification of a germline BRCA1 pathogenic variant (c.5266dupC,
p.Gln1756ProfsTer74) indicates hereditary breast and ovarian cancer (HBOC)
syndrome. This finding has significant implications for the patient and her
biological relatives. Formal genetic counseling is STRONGLY RECOMMENDED for:
  (1) Discussion of cancer risk management, enhanced screening protocols, and
      risk-reduction surgical options for contralateral breast cancer and any
      future gynecologic considerations;
  (2) Cascade testing guidance for first-degree relatives (children, siblings);
      each first-degree relative has a 50% probability of carrying this variant;
  (3) Review of family history consistent with hereditary cancer risk (maternal
      breast cancer at age 45, maternal aunt with ovarian cancer).

CHIEF COMPLAINT:
Ms. Okonkwo is a 58-year-old postmenopausal West African woman referred to gynecologic oncology following new diagnosis of high-grade serous ovarian carcinoma (HGSOC) confirmed on omental core biopsy. She presents today for surgical planning and pre-operative assessment.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:
Ms. Okonkwo initially presented to her primary gynecologist approximately 6 weeks ago with a several-month history of progressive abdominal distension, early satiety, and vague pelvic pressure. She had also noted a 6-pound unintentional weight loss over the preceding 2-3 months, which she attributed initially to dietary changes. She denied any vaginal bleeding. On pelvic exam, bilateral adnexal masses were palpated. CA-125 was obtained and returned markedly elevated at 4,820 U/mL. She underwent CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis, which demonstrated bilateral ovarian masses, peritoneal carcinomatosis with an omental cake, pelvic sidewall adenopathy, ascites, and diaphragmatic implants. No pleural effusions were identified. Retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy appeared confined to the pelvis and lower para-aortic region below the renal vessels.

She was subsequently referred for image-guided omental core needle biopsy, which confirmed HGSOC with PAX8+, WT-1+, and aberrant p53 expression. Germline genetic testing has been completed and revealed a pathogenic BRCA1 variant (c.5266dupC, also known as the 5382insC founder variant). Somatic NGS confirmed TP53 mutation, BRCA1 LOH, with additional alterations including RB1 loss, CCNE1 amplification, MYC gain, and NF1 alteration; the tumor is HRD-positive.

The case was presented at our multidisciplinary tumor board this week. Given the imaging findings — bilateral ovarian/adnexal masses, omental cake, pelvic and lower para-aortic nodal disease, and diaphragmatic implants — she carries Stage IIIC disease. However, the absence of bulky retroperitoneal adenopathy above the renal vessels, no liver parenchymal involvement, and no pleural effusions suggested that primary debulking surgery (PDS) with a goal of complete gross resection could be feasible in an experienced surgical center. The tumor board consensus was to proceed with diagnostic laparoscopy first to formally assess resectability prior to any commitment to laparotomy.

Diagnostic laparoscopy was performed this admission. Findings reviewed in detail below. Fagotti score was calculated at 6: bilateral ovarian masses (+2), omental cake (+2), diaphragmatic involvement (+2). No bowel mesenteric retraction, no stomach/lesser sac involvement, no liver surface disease to score. A Fagotti score of 6 is borderline for primary resection; however, given the nature of the disease distribution and the absence of unresectable upper abdominal pathology, multidisciplinary consensus was to proceed with PDS. The patient has been extensively counseled regarding the anticipated extent of surgery, including possible bowel resection and anastomosis, and she has provided informed consent.

She is here today for final pre-operative assessment and to review all outstanding results before scheduling.

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN:

Ms. Okonkwo is a 58-year-old postmenopausal woman with new diagnosis of FIGO Stage IIIC HGSOC. She carries a germline BRCA1 pathogenic variant (c.5266dupC), which has significant prognostic and therapeutic implications. Her tumor is HRD-positive. She has excellent PS (ECOG 1) and no major comorbidities that would preclude aggressive surgical intervention.

1. PRIMARY DEBULKING SURGERY (PDS):
Following diagnostic laparoscopy and multidisciplinary tumor board review, we will proceed with PDS. The anticipated procedure includes: total abdominal hysterectomy with bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy (TAH-BSO), omentectomy, pelvic and para-aortic lymph node dissection, peritoneal stripping as needed, and likely rectosigmoid resection with primary anastomosis given disease distribution in the pelvis. Cholecystectomy and splenectomy may be required depending on intraoperative findings. The goal is complete gross resection (R0). Patient has been consented for all of the above, including possible colostomy if anastomosis is not safe intraoperatively — she understands and accepts.

Anesthesia pre-assessment reviewed: ASA Class II. EKG normal. Cardiac clearance not required.

2. NUTRITIONAL OPTIMIZATION:
Albumin 3.4 g/dL — mildly low. Pre-surgical nutritional support initiated with high-protein oral supplements for at least 5-7 days pre-op. Dietitian referral placed. Will reassess prior to case.

3. VENOUS THROMBOEMBOLISM PROPHYLAXIS:
Given ovarian cancer diagnosis and planned major abdominal surgery, she will receive standard perioperative VTE prophylaxis per institutional protocol (sequential compression devices intraoperatively, low molecular weight heparin post-op). Extended prophylaxis post-discharge to be determined.

4. ADJUVANT CHEMOTHERAPY PLANNING:
Following surgery, assuming successful cytoreduction, she will be planned for platinum/taxane-based chemotherapy — carboplatin/paclitaxel x6 cycles. Bevacizumab use deferred at this time given anticipated anastomosis (bowel resection); will reassess at post-op visit.

5. MAINTENANCE THERAPY:
Given germline BRCA1 status and HRD-positive tumor, she is an excellent candidate for PARP inhibitor maintenance therapy (olaparib or niraparib) following completion of first-line chemotherapy, provided she achieves a response. This has been introduced to the patient today; detailed discussion will occur at the post-operative and pre-maintenance visits.

6. GENETIC COUNSELING:
Formal genetic counseling referral placed for the patient and to facilitate cascade testing for her three adult children. Her BRCA1 status (5382insC) carries implications for first-degree relatives.

7. FOLLOW-UP:
Scheduled for PDS in approximately 1 week. Pre-op visit with anesthesia arranged. Patient instructed to hold lisinopril morning of surgery per anesthesia preference. She has our oncology team's contact information and knows to call with any new or worsening symptoms in the interim.

Time spent: approximately 75 minutes total, including history, examination, case review, and extensive counseling regarding diagnosis, surgical plan, and future treatment trajectory. Greater than 50% of time spent in counseling.

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT
Post-operative oncology follow-up visit, approximately 5 weeks after primary debulking surgery (PDS) for newly diagnosed high-grade serous ovarian carcinoma.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS
Ms. Okonkwo is a 58-year-old postmenopausal West African woman, G3P3, who presents today for her first post-operative oncology visit following PDS performed approximately 5 weeks ago. She was initially evaluated in GynOnc clinic after presenting with abdominal distension and bloating. Staging CT at that time demonstrated bilateral ovarian masses, large volume ascites (3.2L drained at time of surgery), diffuse peritoneal carcinomatosis with omental cake, and pelvic sidewall lymphadenopathy. CA-125 at diagnosis was markedly elevated at 4,820 U/mL. Omental biopsy confirmed high-grade serous carcinoma (HGSOC), PAX8+, WT-1+, p53 aberrant, Ki-67 80%. Germline testing returned positive for a pathogenic BRCA1 variant (c.5266dupC, also known as 5382insC). Fagotti score was 6 at time of pre-operative assessment, and the decision was made to proceed with upfront PDS.

She underwent primary debulking via midline laparotomy including TAH-BSO, omentectomy, pelvic and para-aortic lymph node dissection, peritoneal stripping, rectosigmoid resection with primary anastomosis, appendectomy, cholecystectomy, and splenectomy. Pathologic stage confirmed pT3c pN1 M0 (FIGO IIIC): bilateral ovarian HGSOC with STIC in the right tube, involvement of omentum, diaphragm, peritoneum, rectosigmoid serosa, and splenic hilum. Four of 18 pelvic lymph nodes and 2 of 14 para-aortic lymph nodes were positive. Estimated blood loss was 1,850 mL. R0 resection was achieved.

Post-operative course was complicated by Grade 2 ileus noted on POD4, managed conservatively with NGT decompression and bowel rest, and a Grade 1 wound seroma. She was ultimately discharged on POD12 after return of bowel function and clinical improvement.

Since discharge, she reports overall improving energy levels, though she remains fatigued. She has been tolerating a regular diet without difficulty. No abdominal pain, no nausea or vomiting. Bowel function has normalized; she is having daily soft formed stools without blood. She denies dysuria, urinary incontinence, or urinary retention. She reports mild incisional soreness with activity, which is improving. She denies fever or chills. No lower extremity swelling or calf pain. Appetite is decreased from baseline but improved compared to immediately post-discharge.

Today's visit focused on: (1) wound and functional recovery assessment, (2) review of final surgical pathology and molecular results, (3) adjuvant treatment planning, (4) genetic counseling referral, and (5) discussion of PARP inhibitor maintenance.

---

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

1. HIGH-GRADE SEROUS OVARIAN CARCINOMA, FIGO IIIC, pT3c pN1 M0, BRCA1 germline mutated, R0 resection achieved
   - Patient is approximately 5 weeks post PDS with excellent functional recovery. ECOG 1. Wound healing appropriately. R0 resection achieved — excellent surgical outcome given disease burden at presentation.
   - CA-125 trending down significantly (4,820 → 847 U/mL), consistent with favorable response to debulking. Will continue to monitor as chemotherapy begins.
   - Plan to initiate adjuvant chemotherapy: Paraplatin AUC 5 / Taxol 175 mg/m2 IV every 21 days x 6 cycles (standard GOG-158 backbone). Target start within the next 1-2 weeks once we confirm adequate counts and functional status.

2. Avastin (Avastin) — DEFERRED
   - Discussed the option of adding bev (Avastin) to the backbone regimen per GOG-0218 data, with potential benefit in high-risk stage III/IV disease. However, given the recent rectosigmoid resection with anastomosis and concern for impaired wound healing and anastomotic integrity, Avastin initiation is deferred. Patient also expressed personal preference against it at this time given these concerns. Will revisit in the maintenance setting if clinically appropriate, though PARP inhibitor is the priority maintenance strategy given BRCA1 status.

3. ADJUVANT CHEMOTHERAPY PRE-TREATMENT WORKUP
   - Baseline labs reviewed and acceptable for chemotherapy start (ANC adequate, Plt 312, Cr 0.82).
   - Mild anemia (Hgb 9.8) — not a contraindication to initiation; will continue iron. Repeat CBC prior to C1 Day 1.
   - Baseline neuropathy: none. Counseled patient on cumulative pacli-related peripheral neuropathy; will assess at each cycle.
   - Antiemetic prophylaxis plan: ondansetron + dexamethasone + fosaprepitant per Paraplatin/Taxol protocol.
   - Compazine PRN breakthrough.

4. BRCA1 GERMLINE RESULT — REFERRAL TO GENETIC COUNSELING
   - Reviewed germline BRCA1 c.5266dupC (pathogenic) result in detail with patient today. Counseled regarding implications for personal cancer surveillance and risk in family members.
   - Formal referral placed to genetic counseling for cascade testing of her three adult children. Patient expressed appropriate understanding and agreed to share results with family.
   - Discussed BRCA1-related increased risk for breast cancer and other BRCA-associated malignancies; breast surveillance to be coordinated with PCP/breast imaging.

5. PARP INHIBITOR MAINTENANCE — PLAN DISCUSSED
   - Following completion of 6 cycles of platinum-taxane chemotherapy, patient is an excellent candidate for PARP inhibitor maintenance given BRCA1 germline mutation. Options discussed include nira (Zejula) and Lynparza (Lynparza); both FDA-approved in this setting.
   - Will finalize choice of agent and dosing at the post-C6 visit based on response, counts, and tolerability during chemotherapy.
   - Patient is engaged and understands the rationale. Counseled regarding common toxicities (thrombocytopenia, anemia, nausea, fatigue for Zejula; similar plus potential for MDS/AML long-term for both).

6. ANEMIA
   - Continue ferrous sulfate 325 mg daily. Recheck CBC at C1 Day 1. If Hgb declines further or symptoms develop, will consider transfusion or erythropoiesis-stimulating agent per guidelines.

7. PSYCHOSOCIAL
   - Patient reports anxiety related to new diagnosis; discussed normalcy of this and importance of support resources. Referral to oncology social work placed. Patient has strong family support.

8. FOLLOW-UP
   - C1 Day 1: Scheduled in approximately 10 days pending pre-chemo labs. Infusion appointment placed.
   - Follow-up in clinic after C1 to assess tolerability.
   - Restaging CT and CA-125 planned after C3.
   - Genetic counseling appointment pending scheduling.
